bills.title: Ending Importation of Laundered Russian Oil Act
bills.status: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.
bills.nexus: H.R. 7095 directly implicates Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC), which administers sanctions on Russian energy under Executive Orders 14024 and 14066, and the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN), which monitors laundering of proceeds through correspondent banking. The bill could mandate new import prohibitions or price cap enforcement mechanisms that OFAC would be required to implement, raising compliance, interagency coordination, and potential evasion-detection burdens for Treasury.

bills.title: Protecting American Taxpayers Act
bills.status: Read the second time. Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 401.
bills.nexus: S. 4378 targets federal tax administration and taxpayer protections, placing it squarely within the jurisdiction of the Internal Revenue Service and the Office of Tax Policy. Treasury must track it for potential implementation burdens on IRS operations and any fiscal risk tied to changes in collections, enforcement authority, or taxpayer rights frameworks.

bills.title: Public Lands Integrity Act
bills.status: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Budget.
bills.nexus: S. 4455 touches Treasury tangentially through the budgetary scoring and mandatory spending implications of any revenue-sharing or land-disposal provisions that could affect federal receipts and offsetting collections tracked by the Office of Tax Policy and the Fiscal Assistant Secretary. The implementation risk centers on whether CBO scoring of land conveyance proceeds triggers pay-as-you-go sequestration obligations that Treasury's Office of Fiscal Service would need to execute.

bills.title: Protecting America’s Small Oil and Gas Producers and Rural Jobs Act
bills.status: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
bills.nexus: S. 4604 directly implicates the Office of Tax Policy and IRS because it would modify tax treatment affecting small oil and gas producers, likely touching depletion allowances, intangible drilling cost deductions, or related provisions under the Internal Revenue Code. Treasury's implementation risk centers on revenue scoring, regulatory guidance burdens on IRS, and ensuring any new preferences are administrable without creating compliance gaps or unintended windfalls.

bills.title: Connected Vehicle Security Act of 2026
bills.status: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.
bills.nexus: S. 4429 would direct the Department of Commerce's National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA) and the FCC to establish security standards for connected vehicles, which touches Treasury only tangentially through its Office of Cybersecurity and Critical Infrastructure Protection (OCCIP) to the extent that automotive financial data or payment systems embedded in connected vehicles could be implicated. Treasury's primary risk angle is that any federal connected-vehicle security framework could impose compliance requirements on financial institutions offering vehicle-integrated payment services or affect OFAC's ability to monitor and restrict data flows involving sanctioned entities accessing U.S. vehicle networks.

bills.title: Access to Fair Financing for Opportunity and Resilient Development Act
bills.status: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.
bills.nexus: S. 3940 appears to touch Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI) Fund programming and potentially New Markets Tax Credit or related Treasury lending authorities that support underserved communities. Treasury's implementation risk centers on any expanded appropriations, certification requirements, or credit subsidy obligations that the CDFI Fund or IRS would need to administer under new statutory mandates.

bills.title: National Fallen Firefighters Memorial Coin Act
bills.status: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S1011)
bills.nexus: S. 4051 authorizes the Treasury Department to mint and issue commemorative coins, directly implicating the U.S. Mint (a Treasury bureau) under its statutory coinage authority (31 U.S.C. 5112). Treasury must manage the coin design, production, and surcharge disbursement to the National Fallen Firefighters Foundation, creating financial and operational execution obligations.

bills.title: Historic Tax Credit Growth and Opportunity Act of 2025
bills.status: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
bills.nexus: S. 1459 directly implicates the Internal Revenue Service and the Office of Tax Policy because it proposes expanding IRC Section 47, the Historic Tax Credit, by increasing the credit rate and modifying eligibility thresholds. Treasury faces implementation risk in promulgating updated regulations and guidance, and revenue risk from the expanded credit's projected cost to the federal fisc.

bills.title: Chip Security Act
bills.status: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.
bills.nexus: S. 1705 would likely implicate Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control and/or the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (CFIUS), given that semiconductor security legislation typically intersects with export controls, supply chain integrity, and national security reviews of foreign access to critical technology. Treasury's implementation risk centers on potential new compliance mandates, screening authorities, or reporting requirements that could expand CFIUS jurisdiction or require coordination with Commerce on controlled chip transactions.

bills.title: SAFE Tax Filing Act of 2025
bills.status: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
bills.nexus: S. 2129 would direct the IRS, housed within Treasury, to develop or expand a free, government-run direct tax filing system, implicating the Commissioner's authority over taxpayer services and modernization under IRC Section 7803. Treasury's implementation risk centers on funding requirements, potential conflict with existing Free File agreements, and IRS system capacity at a time when IT modernization is already under strain.
